domain: Reward Model & Verifier Training Platforms for SLM Reasoning
validation_sources: Technical blogs (Sebastian Raschka, Cameron Wolfe, Stephen Diehl, Subhadip Mitra)

validated_gaps.title: No unified PRM+ORM+GenRM training platform

demand_signals.signal: GenRM (ICLR 2025) shows generative verifiers outperform discriminative ones by large margins (5%->45.3% on algorithmic tasks). Yet no framework integrates GenRM training alongside PRM/ORM.
RLHFlow repo provides separate Math-RM code for PRM and ORM but not GenRM. Users must implement GenRM from scratch.
ThinkPRM outperforms discriminative verifiers using only 1% of PRM800K labels, showing GenRM-style approaches are superior but lack tooling.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Strong research signal but demand is mainly from ML researchers, not mainstream developers. The GenRM approach is clearly superior but not yet widely adopted due to lack of tooling.

validated_gaps.title: No SLM-optimized reward model training toolkit

demand_signals.signal: Unsloth's GRPO tutorial is one of the most popular pages. Shows training reasoning on Qwen 3B with 5GB VRAM. But no RM training support -- only GRPO with rule-based rewards.
Blog post 'Fine-tuning 20B LLMs with RLHF on a 24GB consumer GPU' using TRL+PEFT shows strong demand for consumer-GPU RLHF. But focuses on ORM only, not PRM.
TinyZero (R1-Zero reproduction on small models) had massive HN engagement. Shows strong demand for SLM reasoning training. But uses only rule-based rewards, not trained verifiers.
Multiple 2026 guides on 'Top SLMs' and 'How to train SLMs' -- none cover reward model training specifically.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Very strong demand signal. The SLM reasoning training wave (post-DeepSeek R1) created massive interest but everyone uses GRPO with rule-based rewards because training reward models on consumer hardware is too hard. Huge unmet need.

validated_gaps.title: Custom domain verifier training is extremely manual

demand_signals.signal: Dedicated blog post calling this 'The Verifier Problem Nobody Has Solved'. Identifies sparse rewards, reward hacking, subjectivity, and missing ground truth as blockers for non-math/code RLVR.
Knowledge-to-Verification paper specifically addresses extending RLVR to knowledge-intensive domains, published at ICLR 2026. The paper's existence proves the gap is real.
Fin-PRM paper builds a domain-specific PRM for financial reasoning. Had to build custom pipeline from scratch. No reusable tooling.
'If I were allocating engineering resources for 2026, verifier development would be near the top of the list.' Direct quote from post-training techniques analysis.

demand_signals.source: Medium: 'Extending RLVR Beyond Math and Code'
demand_signals.signal: Multiple blog posts in 2025-2026 addressing this exact gap, proposing workarounds like 'Judge Code' generation.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: This is THE problem in the space. Multiple dedicated articles, papers, and blog posts calling it out. Research labs are building one-off solutions (Fin-PRM, K2V) but no general-purpose tool exists. Highest demand signal of all gaps.

validated_gaps.title: No automated step-labeling pipeline for PRM data

demand_signals.signal: Active learning paper shows PRM training with 20% of annotation cost. Paper's motivation section explicitly describes the annotation cost bottleneck.
Entropy-Driven PRM paper provides step-level supervision 'without expensive human or LLM annotations'. Proves the cost problem is real.

demand_signals.source: Research: 'Free Process Rewards without Process Labels' (2024)
demand_signals.signal: Paper title literally describes the gap -- getting process rewards without process labels. Shows implicit PRM from ORM training.
OpenAI's PRM800K required human experts for 75K questions. Still the gold standard dataset years later because nobody can easily generate equivalent quality data.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Strong academic demand. Multiple papers specifically trying to solve the annotation cost problem. But approaches remain fragmented research prototypes, not productized pipelines.

validated_gaps.title: No end-to-end verifier-guided reasoning training loop for SLMs

demand_signals.signal: DeepLearning.AI launched a 'Reinforcement Fine-Tuning LLMs with GRPO' course. Shows massive demand for end-to-end training but only covers GRPO with rule-based rewards, not full verifier loop.
'Lessons on reproducing R1-like reasoning in small LLMs' -- describes the difficulty of the full training pipeline. Multiple manual steps required.
'How to Fine-Tune Small Language Models to Think with Reinforcement Learning' -- tutorial showing the complexity of the multi-tool pipeline.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Strong demand from practitioners wanting to replicate DeepSeek R1-style training. Current solutions require stitching 3-5 tools together.

validated_gaps.title: No visual/interactive tool for PRM data inspection and debugging

demand_signals.signal: Process Reward Models blog post discusses training and inference but notes difficulty of debugging step-level reward assignments.
Argilla has preference annotation UI but no step-level reward visualization. Gap is visible in their feature set.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate demand. Debugging PRM data quality is a real problem but the user base is small (ML researchers training PRMs). Nice-to-have, not critical.

validated_gaps.title: No reward model benchmarking suite for SLM-specific evaluation

demand_signals.signal: Existing benchmarks focus on general RM quality, not SLM-specific reasoning patterns. No test suites for verifiers on SLM-generated traces.
AURORA introduced UniversalBench for broader PRM evaluation, but still not SLM-focused.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate demand. Benchmarks matter for research credibility but won't drive product adoption on their own.

validated_gaps.title: No tree search + verifier inference toolkit for SLMs

demand_signals.signal: ReasoningGym provides environments for RL with verifiable rewards. Shows demand for reasoning infrastructure.
LLaMA-Berry achieved 96.1% on GSM8K with MCTS + verifier on small models. Demonstrates the value but is research-only code.
OpenR provides PRM-guided search but limited in scope and maturity. Shows demand for production-ready version.
'Inference-time scaling lets us take a relatively small model and boost its performance to rival some of the biggest names'. Direct endorsement of the approach.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Good demand. Inference-time compute scaling is a proven technique for SLMs but no production toolkit exists. The value proposition is clear: small model + verifier + search = big model performance.

validated_gaps.title: No multi-domain PRM transfer learning toolkit

demand_signals.signal: VersaPRM paper shows math PRMs have poor performance in other domains (only 1.3% gain in Law). Proves domain adaptation is needed.
AURORA released Universal-PRM-7B but no toolkit for fine-tuning it to new domains.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate-high demand. The problem is validated by research (VersaPRM) but the user base for domain adaptation is currently small.

validated_gaps.title: No synthetic reasoning trace generator for reward model training

demand_signals.signal: VersaPRM uses weak/strong completer consistency for auto-labeling. Shows the technique works but requires custom implementation.
Uses entropy-driven sampling to generate diverse reasoning steps. Another custom implementation of the same need.
Math-Shepherd's automatic annotation framework is widely cited. Shows demand for automated data generation.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Strong research demand. Multiple papers independently solve this problem for their specific use case, proving the general tool is missing.

validated_gaps.title: No reward model distillation pipeline (large RM to small RM)

demand_signals.signal: Paper explicitly addresses using smaller models as reward models. Proves the direction is viable.
Skywork released a 1.5B PRM alongside the 7B version. Shows demand for small verifiers.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate demand. Small verifiers are useful but knowledge distillation is a general ML technique -- not clear a RM-specific tool adds enough value.

validated_gaps.title: No managed/cloud platform for reward model training

demand_signals.signal: Data labeling services (Cogito, Aya Data) offer RLHF dataset preparation as a service. Shows demand for managed solutions.
Labelbox has RLHF implementation guides but no managed RM training. Gap in their offering.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate demand. Enterprise/SaaS opportunity but most reward model training users are ML engineers comfortable with code. Timing may be early.

validated_gaps.title: No code reasoning verifier beyond test execution

demand_signals.signal: HERMES integrates formal verification into reasoning but for math. Code formal verification for LLM training is unexplored territory.
All code RLVR relies on test execution. No step-level code reasoning verification exists.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Low-moderate demand. Test execution works well enough for code. Step-level code verification is technically very hard and may not be worth the complexity.

validated_gaps.title: No reward model version control and experiment tracking

demand_signals.signal: No RM-specific experiment tracking tools exist. But W&B/MLflow can be adapted.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Low demand. General MLOps tools suffice. Not enough differentiation to warrant a standalone tool.

validated_gaps.title: No plug-and-play verifier marketplace/registry

demand_signals.signal: Models exist on HuggingFace but no verifier-specific search, compatibility info, or deployment guides.
GitHub repo curating reward models shows demand for discovery but is just a list, not a registry.

validated_gaps.validation_notes: Moderate demand. HuggingFace is the de facto registry. A verifier-specific layer on top could add value but faces platform risk.
